Title: Natural genetic variation in Arabidopsis thaliana defense metabolism genes modulates field fitness

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Activated glucosinolate hydrolysis products (nitriles vs isothiocyanates)
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Guides activated glucosinolate breakdown toward nitrile formation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ESP was one of the naturally polymorphic core loci incorporated into the synthetic Col-0 laboratory population. 2. A functional ESP allele was introduced by 35S overexpression and is described as guiding formation of activated glucosinolates to nitriles. 3. The study used ESP-containing genotypes as part of the field fitness test of glucosinolate pathway variation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: 35S overexpression in Col-0-derived background; field glucosinolate/fitness phenotyping
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Vacuole-associated glucosinolate activation system after tissue disruption
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field environments UCD2012, UWY2011, UWY2012; herbivory/pesticide treatment context
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter defensive hydrolysis product output and thereby change ecological interactions and fitness depending on environment
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be used to redirect glucosinolate activation products toward nitriles in defense-metabolism engineering, but ecological outcomes are environment-dependent
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified here as Common Gene because the paper describes ESP as a co-factor rather than a catalytic pathway enzyme or transcriptional regulator; although included in the synthetic population, the Results emphasize pathway-wide genotype effects more than a dedicated single-gene dissection for ESP.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Controls methionine-derived side-chain elongation that determines whether 3-carbon or 4-carbon aliphatic glucosinolate precursors are produced
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Elongated amino acid precursors favoring 4C side chains
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Determines side-chain length of the target glucosinolate products
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for 3C versus 4C chain-length determination
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: plant defense against herbivores and pathogens
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf tissue; glucosinolates stored in vacuoles
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functional MAM1 promotes 4C glucosinolate accumulation, whereas nonfunctional gsm1 leads to predominant 3C glucosinolates
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MAM1 natural variation was recreated in the Col-0 background using the gsm1 EMS mutant. 2. In the field, functional versus nonfunctional MAM1 always predicted glucosinolate chain length as in laboratory studies. 3. MAM1-containing glucosinolate genotypes contributed to environmentally dependent fitness differences in the field.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: EMS mutant gsm1; isogenic crosses; field metabolite profiling by HPLC; genotype-phenotype comparison across environments
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Alters glucosinolate chemotype and associated ecological interactions, with fitness effects depending on environment
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering glucosinolate chain-length chemotypes, but deployment should consider environment-specific fitness consequences
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper refers to the nonfunctional MAM1 allele as gsm1 in some genotype names.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygenates methylthio glucosinolates to methylsulfinyl glucosinolates
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies oxidized aliphatic glucosinolate intermediates for downstream side-chain modification
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: plant defense against herbivores and pathogens
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf tissue; glucosinolates stored in vacuoles
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expected to reduce MT-to-MSO conversion when nonfunctional, but field plants showed no measurable MT accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GSOX1 is a naturally variable aliphatic glucosinolate pathway gene included in the synthetic population. 2. Laboratory studies predicted increased methylthio glucosinolate accumulation in gsox1 mutants. 3. In the field, this expected phenotype was not measurable, likely because of functional redundancy from GSOX2 and GSOX4.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA mutant; field HPLC glucosinolate profiling; comparison to published lab phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Functional redundancy may mask engineering effects in field conditions
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited as a standalone engineering target because redundant paralogs can buffer phenotype in the field
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Field behavior differed from lab expectations; redundancy at the GSOX locus was explicitly discussed.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygenates methylthio glucosinolates to methylsulfinyl glucosinolates
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies oxidized aliphatic glucosinolate intermediates for downstream side-chain modification
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: plant defense against herbivores and pathogens
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf tissue; glucosinolates stored in vacuoles
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expected to reduce MT-to-MSO conversion when nonfunctional, but field plants showed no measurable MT accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GSOX3 is a naturally variable aliphatic glucosinolate pathway gene tested in the synthetic population. 2. Laboratory studies predicted increased methylthio glucosinolate accumulation in gsox3 mutants. 3. In the field, no measurable MT accumulation was observed, likely due to redundancy with GSOX2 and GSOX4.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Insertion mutant CSHL_GT13906; field HPLC glucosinolate profiling; comparison to published lab phenotypes
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Phenotypic effects can be buffered by paralog redundancy
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Standalone manipulation may be insufficient unless redundant GSOX family members are jointly targeted
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper explicitly states field and laboratory data differ for GSOX1 and GSOX3.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts methylsulfinyl glucosinolates into alkenyl glucosinolates such as allyl and but-3-enyl glucosinolates
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms downstream alkenyl target glucosinolate chemotypes
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: plant defense against herbivores and pathogens
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf tissue; glucosinolates stored in vacuoles
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functional AOP2 causes production of alkenyl glucosinolates and strongly modulates total aliphatic glucosinolate accumulation and field fitness in an environment-dependent manner
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AOP2 natural variation was mimicked by introducing a functional allele into Col-0, which naturally carries a nonfunctional allele. 2. All lines harboring functional AOP2 produced alkenyl glucosinolates in the field, consistent with lab studies. 3. AOP2 genotypes showed marked environment-dependent variation in total aliphatic glucosinolates, herbivory response, and fitness. 4. AOP2 is epistatic to GSOH because GSOH acts on the AOP2 product but-3-enyl glucosinolate.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: 35S:AOP2 complementation/overexpression line; field HPLC metabolite profiling; multi-environment field fitness and herbivory measurements
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can shift chemotype and ecological performance dramatically; genotype can be high fitness in one environment and low fitness in another
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for engineering glucosinolate chemotype, but should be deployed with careful environment-specific evaluation and consideration of epistasis with GSOH
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Col-0 carries a natural knockout of AOP2; functional AOP2 is necessary for GSOH-dependent hydroxy-alkenyl product formation.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms hydroxylated terminal glucosinolate chemotype
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant for hydroxylated butenyl glucosinolate formation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: plant defense against herbivores and pathogens
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf tissue; glucosinolates stored in vacuoles
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functional GSOH leads to production of 2-hydroxy-but-3-enyl glucosinolate when AOP2-derived substrate is present
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GSOH was included as a naturally polymorphic pathway gene in the synthetic population. 2. In the field, a functional GSOH always led to production of 2-hydroxy-but-3-enyl glucosinolate from but-3-enyl glucosinolate. 3. GSOH function depends on upstream AOP2 activity because its substrate is an AOP2 product.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA mutant SALK_098807; field HPLC glucosinolate profiling; genotype combinations demonstrating epistasis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Effect depends on upstream chemotype background and thus may be context dependent in breeding or engineering
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for fine-tuning downstream hydroxylated glucosinolate chemotypes, especially in combination with AOP2

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Positive regulator of aliphatic glucosinolate pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major positive regulator; stronger individual effect than MYB29 and essential with MYB29 for full aliphatic glucosinolate accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: plant defense against herbivores and pathogens
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf tissue; glucosinolates stored in vacuoles
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of MYB28 causes almost complete reduction in long-chain aliphatic glucosinolates and about 60% reduction in short-chain aliphatic glucosinolates; contributes to environment-dependent fitness effects
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB28 is a core naturally polymorphic regulator controlling aliphatic glucosinolate accumulation. 2. In the field, nonfunctional MYB28 caused almost complete loss of long-chain aliphatic glucosinolates and about 60% reduction of short-chain glucosinolates, matching lab observations. 3. MYB28/MYB29 double mutants showed almost complete loss of all aliphatic glucosinolates. 4. MYB28-containing genotypes displayed environment-dependent differences in herbivory and fitness.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA knockout SALK_136312; genotype combinations including double mutant with MYB29; field HPLC metabolite profiling; multi-environment fitness analysis
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced defense metabolite accumulation can alter herbivory and fitness in an environment-specific manner
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong lever for modulating overall aliphatic glucosinolate accumulation, but pleiotropic ecological and fitness consequences require context-specific deployment
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Single knockout effect is larger than MYB29; double knockout with MYB29 nearly abolishes aliphatic glucosinolates.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Positive regulator of aliphatic glucosinolate pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Positive regulator with a weaker single-mutant effect than MYB28 but jointly essential with MYB28 for near-complete pathway output
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: plant defense against herbivores and pathogens
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf tissue; glucosinolates stored in vacuoles
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of MYB29 causes about 40% reduction in short-chain aliphatic glucosinolates with no significant reduction in long-chain glucosinolates; contributes to environment-dependent fitness effects
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYB29 is a core naturally polymorphic regulator of aliphatic glucosinolate accumulation. 2. In the field, nonfunctional MYB29 reduced short-chain aliphatic glucosinolates by about 40% with no significant reduction in long-chain glucosinolates, as expected from lab studies. 3. Combined loss of MYB28 and MYB29 caused almost complete loss of all aliphatic glucosinolates. 4. MYB29-containing genotypes contributed to environment-dependent herbivory and fitness variation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant SM.34316; double-mutant comparison with MYB28; field HPLC metabolite profiling; multi-environment fitness analysis
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Changing MYB29-dependent defense metabolism can influence ecological performance differently across environments
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for partial tuning of aliphatic glucosinolate levels and chemotype strength, especially when combined with MYB28 manipulation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Single knockout effect is weaker and more restricted than MYB28, but the two regulators are jointly necessary for full pathway accumulation.
